note: Historical precedents are factual, dated, well-documented events — presented as fact. Possibility chains are explicitly hedged: they describe structural/economic relationships observed historically, not predictions of what will happen this time. Matching is word-boundary keyword scoring against headline title + summary, mirroring taxonomy/aspects.json's approach — keep this file in sync with os/global-intelligence-site/data/ripple_effects.json.

playbooks.name: Persian Gulf oil supply shock

historical_precedents.what_happened: Iran and Iraq attacked oil tankers transiting the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz, and Iran repeatedly threatened to close the strait.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Shipping insurance war-risk premiums in the Gulf rose sharply, and the U.S. Navy began escorting reflagged Kuwaiti tankers in 1987 to keep the strait open.

historical_precedents.event: Drone and missile attack on Saudi Aramco's Abqaiq and Khurais facilities
historical_precedents.what_happened: The attack (which Saudi Arabia and the U.S. blamed on Iran, a claim Iran denied) temporarily knocked out roughly half of Saudi Arabia's oil output, about 5% of global supply.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Brent crude jumped about 15% in a single day — the largest one-day oil price increase since the 1990–91 Gulf War.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait removed a large share of Gulf oil exports from the market and raised fears of a wider regional war.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Oil prices roughly doubled in the months following the invasion before falling back once the war ended and supply concerns eased.

possibility_chains: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil is estimated to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, so a sustained blockage or major disruption there has historically driven crude oil prices sharply higher.

Because ammonia-based fertilizer production is natural-gas intensive, and natural gas prices often move together with oil, a prolonged energy price spike has historically fed through into higher fertilizer costs over the following months.
Fertilizer is a major input cost in food production, so energy shocks of this kind have historically been followed — often with a lag — by higher food prices.
Shipping insurers have historically raised war-risk premiums for vessels transiting the Gulf during periods of heightened tension, adding to the landed cost of oil and other cargo.

playbooks.name: OPEC+ production decision

historical_precedents.what_happened: Arab OPEC members embargoed oil exports to the U.S. and other countries seen as supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Oil prices roughly quadrupled, from around $3 to around $12 a barrel, and triggered widespread fuel shortages and rationing in the U.S. and Europe.

historical_precedents.event: OPEC+ price war and COVID-19 demand collapse
historical_precedents.what_happened: A brief Saudi–Russia price war combined with a collapse in travel and industrial demand during the early COVID-19 pandemic to create a severe oil supply glut.
historical_precedents.market_impact: U.S. crude futures (WTI) briefly traded below zero — around –$37 a barrel — for the first time in history, as storage capacity ran out.

possibility_chains: Coordinated OPEC+ output cuts have historically tightened supply and pushed crude oil prices higher over the following weeks, though the size of the move has varied a great deal by episode.
Higher crude prices have historically raised transportation and shipping costs, which can feed through into the price of goods that travel long distances before reaching consumers.
Sustained higher energy costs have historically weighed on energy-intensive manufacturing sectors, such as chemicals, fertilizer, and steel.

playbooks.name: Critical shipping route disruption

historical_precedents.event: The container ship Ever Given ran aground and blocked the Suez Canal
historical_precedents.what_happened: The vessel blocked the canal for about six days, backing up hundreds of ships that normally use the route connecting Europe and Asia.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The blockage was widely estimated to be holding up several billion dollars of trade per day, and briefly pushed up oil prices and freight rates as shippers weighed rerouting.

historical_precedents.event: Houthi attacks on commercial shipping in the Red Sea
historical_precedents.what_happened: Yemen's Houthi movement began attacking commercial vessels in the Red Sea and Bab-el-Mandeb strait, a route that normally carries a large share of Europe–Asia container traffic via the Suez Canal.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Many major shipping lines rerouted around Africa's Cape of Good Hope, adding roughly 10–14 days to typical Asia–Europe voyages and pushing container freight rates sharply higher on affected routes.

possibility_chains: Disruption to a major shipping chokepoint has historically forced vessels onto longer alternate routes, raising fuel and time costs that carriers have often passed on as higher freight rates.
Longer transit times have historically strained "just-in-time" supply chains, sometimes causing component and inventory shortages for manufacturers who rely on predictable delivery schedules.
Higher freight and insurance costs on a major trade route have historically taken weeks to months to show up in the retail price of imported goods, given typical shipping and inventory lead times.

playbooks.name: Black Sea grain and fertilizer export disruption

historical_precedents.event: Russia's invasion of Ukraine disrupted Black Sea grain shipments
historical_precedents.what_happened: Russia and Ukraine together were, before the war, among the world's largest wheat exporters, and Russia and Belarus were major exporters of potash, a key fertilizer ingredient. The invasion and a Russian naval blockade halted most Ukrainian grain exports for months.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Global wheat and other grain prices spiked to multi-year highs in the months after the invasion, and fertilizer prices — already elevated from high natural gas costs — rose further, squeezing farmers worldwide.

historical_precedents.event: Russia withdrew from the Black Sea Grain Initiative
historical_precedents.what_happened: Russia ended its participation in the UN-brokered deal that had allowed Ukrainian grain exports to resume via a protected Black Sea corridor.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Wheat and corn futures rose in the immediate aftermath on renewed supply concerns, though the size and durability of the move varied with harvest conditions elsewhere.

possibility_chains: Disruption to a major grain-exporting region has historically pushed global wheat, corn, or other staple crop prices higher, particularly when it coincides with weak harvests elsewhere.

Because fertilizer is a significant cost for farmers, disruption to a major fertilizer-exporting region has historically raised planting costs and, with a lag of a growing season or more, crop prices.

Countries that rely heavily on imported grain — particularly in North Africa and the Middle East — have historically been the most exposed to food-price and food-security stress from this kind of shock.

playbooks.name: Major tariff escalation / trade war

historical_precedents.what_happened: The U.S. imposed tariffs on hundreds of billions of dollars of Chinese goods, and China retaliated with its own tariffs, including on U.S. agricultural exports.
historical_precedents.market_impact: U.S. soybean exports to China fell sharply, and the U.S. government paid out tens of billions of dollars in subsidies to farmers hurt by the retaliation; global manufacturers reported higher input costs and supply-chain uncertainty through the period.

historical_precedents.what_happened: The U.S. raised tariffs on over 20,000 imported goods to some of the highest levels in a century; Canada, France, and other major trading partners retaliated with their own tariffs within months.
historical_precedents.market_impact: World trade volumes fell by roughly two-thirds over the following four years; widely cited by economists as having deepened and prolonged the Great Depression globally, not just in the U.S. — the clearest historical example that a major tariff escalation's damage isn't confined to the two countries directly involved.

historical_precedents.event: China's rare earth export restrictions on Japan
historical_precedents.what_happened: After Japan detained a Chinese trawler captain following a collision near the disputed Senkaku/Diaoyu Islands, China informally slowed rare earth mineral shipments to Japan without declaring a formal ban.
historical_precedents.market_impact: China's rare earth exports fell roughly 77% year-on-year by September 2011, and global prices spiked several-fold (cerium oxide rose from around $4.70/kg in April 2010 to around $36/kg that October). The U.S. filed a WTO case in 2012; the WTO ruled against China in 2014, and China dropped its export quotas in 2015 — but the episode had already pushed Japan and other importers to diversify sourcing and boost recycling.

possibility_chains: New tariffs have historically raised the cost of the affected imported goods, at least part of which has often been passed on to consumers or absorbed as lower profit margins by importers.
Retaliatory tariffs targeting a country's agricultural or manufacturing exports have historically hurt the specific industries and regions most exposed to that export market.

Extended periods of tariff uncertainty have historically led some companies to shift or diversify supply chains toward other countries, a process that has typically played out over one to several years, not weeks.

playbooks.name: Semiconductor export controls

historical_precedents.event: U.S. export controls on advanced semiconductors and chipmaking equipment to China
historical_precedents.what_happened: The U.S. restricted sales of advanced AI chips and the equipment used to manufacture leading-edge semiconductors to Chinese customers, later tightening the rules further.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Affected chipmakers reported lost China revenue in subsequent earnings, and China accelerated its own domestic chipmaking investment; some equipment makers had to write down inventory that could no longer be shipped.

historical_precedents.event: Japan's export controls on South Korea
historical_precedents.what_happened: Amid a diplomatic dispute over WWII-era forced-labor compensation rulings, Japan imposed export-license requirements (with roughly a 90-day review) on three materials critical to semiconductor and display manufacturing — fluorinated polyimide, photoresist, and hydrogen fluoride — that South Korea sourced 84.5% and 93.2% of (for polyimide and photoresist respectively) from Japan.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Samsung and SK Hynix, which together accounted for roughly 72% of global DRAM output at the time, faced procurement uncertainty; Samsung reported a 12.4% year-on-year operating profit decline in Q3 2019, partly linked to the disruption. South Korea responded by diversifying sourcing to Belgium, the U.S., and Taiwan and accelerating domestic material production — showing that critical-material export controls, not just chip export bans, are a lever any technologically advanced exporter can use, not only the U.S.

possibility_chains: Export controls on a critical technology have historically prompted the restricted country to accelerate investment in domestic alternatives, a process that has typically taken years rather than months to meaningfully close the gap.
Companies that derive significant revenue from the restricted market have historically seen near-term earnings impact disclosed in their next quarterly results.

Tighter controls on one link in a global supply chain (such as chipmaking equipment) have historically had ripple effects on downstream industries that depend on the restricted technology, including automakers and consumer electronics makers.

playbooks.name: Critical component / chip shortage

historical_precedents.what_happened: A combination of pandemic-related factory shutdowns, a surge in demand for consumer electronics, and automakers' own production cuts early in the pandemic left the industry short of the chips needed for modern vehicles.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Automakers worldwide cut production and, in some cases, shipped vehicles without certain chip-dependent features (like some infotainment options) to be added later; new and used car prices rose noticeably during the shortage.

possibility_chains: A shortage of a critical, widely-used component has historically forced manufacturers across unrelated industries to cut production simultaneously, since the same component often goes into many different products.
Component shortages have historically taken multiple quarters to resolve, since expanding semiconductor manufacturing capacity typically requires long lead times.
Prices for finished goods that depend on the scarce component have historically risen during shortages, both from higher input costs and from constrained supply meeting steady demand.

playbooks.name: Central bank interest rate hikes

historical_precedents.event: U.S. Federal Reserve's fastest rate-hiking cycle in decades
historical_precedents.what_happened: The Fed raised its benchmark interest rate from near zero to over 5% in about 16 months, responding to the highest inflation in roughly 40 years.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The average 30-year U.S. mortgage rate roughly doubled from around 3% to over 7%, home sales slowed sharply, and high-growth technology stocks — more sensitive to rate changes — fell substantially more than the broader market in 2022.

historical_precedents.event: Fed Chair Paul Volcker's rate hikes to break double-digit inflation
historical_precedents.what_happened: The Fed raised interest rates to nearly 20% to bring runaway inflation under control.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The policy triggered a deep recession and a sharp rise in unemployment before inflation was brought down over the following years.

historical_precedents.event: European Central Bank rate hikes under Jean-Claude Trichet
historical_precedents.what_happened: The ECB raised its benchmark rate twice in 2011 (to 1.25% in April, then 1.5% in July) to fight headline inflation, even as the Eurozone sovereign debt crisis was worsening — Portugal's debt was downgraded to junk the same day as the July hike.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Peripheral Eurozone bond yields (Italy, Spain, Portugal, Greece) surged in the following months as the debt crisis deepened; the hikes are widely regarded in hindsight as a policy mistake, and the ECB reversed course with cuts in November and December 2011 after Mario Draghi succeeded Trichet as president — a case where a rate-hike decision, unlike the Fed episodes above, is broadly seen as having worsened rather than merely responded to a crisis.

possibility_chains: Higher central bank interest rates have historically raised borrowing costs across the economy — mortgages, auto loans, and business credit — with mortgage and housing markets typically among the most sensitive.

Rate hikes have historically pressured stock valuations, particularly for growth-oriented companies whose profits are expected further in the future, since higher rates reduce the present value of those future earnings.
Aggressive rate-hiking cycles have historically raised the risk of an economic slowdown or recession, though the size and timing of that effect has varied a great deal across different hiking cycles.

playbooks.name: Central bank interest rate cuts

historical_precedents.event: Fed rate cuts during the global financial crisis
historical_precedents.what_happened: As the housing market collapsed and major financial institutions came under stress, the Fed cut its benchmark rate from over 5% to near zero over about 15 months.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Despite the cuts, U.S. stocks fell sharply through the crisis (the S&P 500 lost roughly half its value peak-to-trough) as the rate cuts worked to cushion, not prevent, the broader financial and economic damage.

historical_precedents.event: Emergency Fed rate cuts at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic
historical_precedents.what_happened: The Fed cut rates to near zero within weeks as lockdowns froze large parts of the economy.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Along with massive fiscal stimulus, the cuts were followed by a rapid stock market recovery and, over the following two years, a surge in inflation as demand rebounded faster than supply chains could.

historical_precedents.event: Bank of Japan introduces negative interest rates
historical_precedents.what_happened: In a surprise 5–4 vote on January 29, 2016 (effective February 16), the BOJ set a rate of -0.1% on a portion of financial institutions' reserves, reacting to a downgraded inflation outlook driven by falling oil prices — a first for a major central bank outside the Eurozone.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Nikkei jumped as much as 3% and 10-year JGB yields fell over 9 basis points (later turning negative for the first time) on the announcement, but the yen's initial ~2% slide against the dollar reversed within weeks as safe-haven flows and skepticism about the policy's effectiveness pushed it sharply stronger instead — a reminder that a central bank's intended market reaction doesn't always hold once initial positioning unwinds.

possibility_chains: Lower interest rates have historically made borrowing cheaper, which has often supported home buying, business investment, and stock valuations over time.

Rate cuts made in response to genuine economic distress (as opposed to routine policy adjustment) have historically coincided with continued near-term market weakness, since the cuts are a response to bad conditions, not an immediate fix for them.

Very low rates sustained for an extended period have historically been followed, with a lag, by higher inflation once demand recovers — though the size of that effect has depended heavily on other conditions at the time.

playbooks.name: Sharp currency devaluation / currency crisis

historical_precedents.what_happened: Thailand's decision to float its currency, the baht, after months of speculative pressure triggered a wave of currency devaluations across Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, and other Asian economies.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Several regional currencies lost half or more of their value against the U.S. dollar within months, stock markets across the region fell sharply, and multiple countries required IMF bailout programs.

historical_precedents.what_happened: The Turkish lira came under severe pressure amid high inflation, large foreign-currency debts, and a diplomatic dispute with the U.S.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The lira lost roughly 30–40% of its value against the U.S. dollar over the year, sharply raising the cost of imports and foreign-currency debt for Turkish businesses.

possibility_chains: A sharp currency devaluation has historically made imported goods, including fuel and food, significantly more expensive for consumers and businesses in the affected country.

Companies and governments with large debts denominated in a foreign currency (commonly the U.S. dollar) have historically faced sharply higher effective debt burdens when their local currency devalues.

A currency crisis in one emerging-market economy has historically raised investor caution toward other economies seen as having similar vulnerabilities, sometimes spreading pressure regionally even without a direct economic link.

playbooks.name: Cyberattack on critical infrastructure

historical_precedents.what_happened: A ransomware attack forced Colonial Pipeline, which carries roughly half of the fuel used on the U.S. East Coast, to shut down its pipeline for about six days as a precaution.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The shutdown caused fuel shortages and panic buying across the U.S. Southeast, with some areas seeing gas stations run dry and average gasoline prices reaching a multi-year high at the time; the company paid a multimillion-dollar ransom, part of which the FBI later recovered.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Malware disguised as ransomware, later attributed by Western governments to Russian military hackers targeting Ukraine, spread far beyond its intended target and crippled the IT systems of multinational companies including shipping giant Maersk, pharmaceutical company Merck, and Ukraine's own government agencies, banks, and power utilities.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Maersk had to reinstall its entire global IT infrastructure and briefly ran port terminal operations manually; total global damages were later estimated at over $10 billion, making it one of the most costly cyberattacks in history.

possibility_chains: A cyberattack that forces critical infrastructure offline (pipelines, ports, power grids) has historically caused localized shortages and price spikes in the directly affected region, even when the underlying supply elsewhere is unaffected.
High-profile infrastructure cyberattacks have historically prompted governments and companies to announce increased cybersecurity spending and, sometimes, new regulation in the following months.
Ransom payments and recovery costs from major attacks have historically drawn attention to cyber-insurance markets and pricing for the affected industry.

playbooks.name: Natural disaster hitting a manufacturing hub

historical_precedents.event: Japan's Tōhoku earthquake, tsunami, and Fukushima nuclear disaster
historical_precedents.what_happened: The disaster killed thousands of people and forced widespread factory shutdowns across Japan, which was and remains a major supplier of automotive parts and specialized electronic components.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Global automakers, including outside Japan, had to slow or halt production for weeks due to parts shortages, and some categories of electronic components saw temporary global shortages and price increases.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Months of monsoon flooding submerged major industrial estates north of Bangkok that housed a large share of the world's hard-disk-drive manufacturing along with numerous automotive-parts factories, forcing an extended shutdown of dozens of plants.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Global hard-drive prices roughly doubled for several months due to the shortage, and several Japanese automakers had to cut production at plants worldwide because of parts sourced from the flooded factories.

possibility_chains: A natural disaster affecting a region that supplies a specialized, hard-to-substitute component has historically caused production disruptions for manufacturers worldwide, not just locally, given how concentrated some supply chains are geographically.

Disruptions of this kind have historically prompted affected companies to review and diversify their supplier base over the following months and years, though meaningful diversification has typically taken longer than the disruption itself.

Recovery timelines have historically depended heavily on how specialized and geographically concentrated the affected manufacturing capacity was — highly specialized hubs (e.g. certain semiconductor fabrication) have taken longer to recover than more easily relocated production.

playbooks.name: Major storm hitting Gulf Coast energy infrastructure

historical_precedents.what_happened: The storms damaged a large share of U.S. Gulf of Mexico oil and gas production and refining capacity, some of which stayed offline for months.
historical_precedents.market_impact: U.S. gasoline prices jumped to record highs at the time, with some regions experiencing temporary shortages, and crude oil prices also rose on the loss of Gulf production.

possibility_chains: A major storm that damages Gulf Coast refineries or offshore production has historically caused a temporary spike in regional (and sometimes national) fuel prices, with the size of the spike depending on how much capacity is affected and for how long.

Refinery outages have historically had a bigger effect on gasoline and diesel prices specifically than on crude oil prices, since refining capacity (not just crude supply) is the bottleneck during these events.
Recovery time has historically depended on the extent of physical damage — flooding and wind damage to a refinery have taken much longer to repair than a precautionary shutdown with no direct damage.

playbooks.name: Major port or dockworker strike

historical_precedents.what_happened: A labor dispute led to a roughly 10-day shutdown of major U.S. West Coast ports, which handle a large share of U.S.–Asia trade, prompting the U.S. government to invoke the Taft-Hartley Act to reopen them.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The shutdown created a large backlog of ships waiting to unload, and analysts estimated it took months for the resulting cargo backlog to fully clear.

historical_precedents.event: East Coast and Gulf Coast port strike
historical_precedents.what_happened: Dockworkers at major U.S. East and Gulf Coast ports went on strike over pay and automation, halting container traffic at dozens of ports for several days before a tentative agreement was reached.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Retailers and manufacturers who had front-loaded inventory ahead of the anticipated strike were less affected than in past disruptions, but analysts warned a longer strike could have caused shortages of goods ranging from bananas to auto parts.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Workers at the Port of Felixstowe, which handles roughly a third of Britain's container trade, staged repeated multi-day strikes over pay that failed to keep pace with inflation — the port's largest strike action in decades.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Container ships were diverted to other European ports, adding delays and cost to goods moving in and out of the UK during an already-strained holiday shopping season.

possibility_chains: A major port shutdown has historically created a cargo backlog that takes considerably longer to clear than the strike itself lasted, since ships and containers pile up faster than a reopened port can process them.

Industries that rely on just-in-time delivery of imported parts (such as auto manufacturing) have historically been more exposed to port disruptions than industries that hold larger buffer inventories.
Perishable goods that move through affected ports (produce, for example) have historically been the most immediately and visibly affected, since they cannot simply wait out a prolonged disruption.

playbooks.name: Sanctions imposed on a major economy

historical_precedents.event: Western sanctions on Russia following its invasion of Ukraine
historical_precedents.what_happened: The U.S., EU, and allies imposed sweeping sanctions on Russia's financial system, energy exports, and access to Western technology, and froze a large share of Russia's foreign-currency reserves.
historical_precedents.market_impact: European natural gas prices spiked to record highs as Russian pipeline gas supply to Europe fell sharply over the following year, and European governments spent heavily to secure alternative energy supplies and shield consumers from higher bills.

historical_precedents.event: International sanctions on Iran over its nuclear program
historical_precedents.what_happened: A series of U.S. and international sanctions restricted Iran's ability to sell oil and access the global banking system.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Iranian oil exports fell sharply, though global oil prices were also affected by supply from other producers filling the gap; Iran's own currency and economy experienced significant strain during the most restrictive sanctions periods.

possibility_chains: Sanctions targeting a major energy or commodity exporter have historically tightened global supply of that commodity, which has often (though not always, depending on how much other producers can offset the loss) pushed prices higher.

Sanctioned countries have historically sought alternative trading partners and payment systems over time, a process that has typically reduced — but not eliminated — the sanctions' economic impact the longer they remain in place.
Countries or companies that depended heavily on trade with the sanctioned economy have historically faced their own adjustment costs, not just the sanctioned party.

playbooks.name: Pandemic-driven supply chain disruption

historical_precedents.what_happened: Repeated lockdowns, factory closures, and worker shortages across major manufacturing and shipping hubs — combined with a sharp shift in consumer demand toward physical goods — overwhelmed global supply chains.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Shipping container costs rose to many times their pre-pandemic levels at the peak, ports experienced record vessel backlogs, and shortages ranged from semiconductors to home appliances to basic goods, contributing to the highest inflation in decades across much of the world by 2022.

possibility_chains: A widespread public-health disruption that closes factories or ports in a major manufacturing region has historically caused shortages of goods that depend on that region, with the size of the effect scaling with how concentrated the affected production is.

Combined supply disruption and a shift in consumer spending patterns has historically produced sharper price increases than either factor alone would, since demand and supply moved in opposite directions at the same time.
Supply-chain disruptions of this scale have historically taken well over a year to substantially normalize, given how interconnected global manufacturing and shipping networks are.

playbooks.name: Major drought or heatwave affecting crop yields

historical_precedents.what_happened: The worst U.S. drought in roughly half a century damaged corn and soybean crops across the Midwest during a critical growing period.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Corn and soybean futures prices reached record highs at the time, raising costs for livestock feed and, with a lag, for meat, dairy, and processed food prices.

historical_precedents.what_happened: An extreme heatwave and wildfires severely damaged Russia's wheat crop, prompting the government to ban wheat exports for several months.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Global wheat prices roughly doubled over the following months, contributing to food-price inflation that some researchers linked to social unrest in several wheat-importing countries in the following year.

historical_precedents.event: East Africa drought and Somali famine
historical_precedents.what_happened: The worst drought in the region in decades destroyed crops and livestock across Somalia, Ethiopia, and Kenya; the United Nations formally declared famine in parts of Somalia in July 2011, the first such declaration in nearly three decades.
historical_precedents.market_impact: An estimated 260,000 people died in Somalia alone, roughly half of them children under five, and millions were displaced across the region — a reminder that crop-yield shocks in the world's poorest, import-dependent regions have historically produced humanitarian catastrophe rather than the price-inflation effects seen in wealthier grain-exporting economies.

possibility_chains: A severe drought or heatwave affecting a major agricultural exporting region has historically reduced crop yields and raised global prices for that crop, with the size of the effect depending on how much of global supply the affected region represents.
Higher crop prices have historically fed through, with a lag of months, into higher prices for livestock feed and then meat and dairy products, since grain is a major input to animal agriculture.

Countries that rely heavily on imports of the affected crop — rather than growing it domestically — have historically been the most exposed to resulting food-price increases and, in some documented cases, food-security stress.

playbooks.name: Sudden stock market crash

historical_precedents.what_happened: US stock markets fell more than 20% in a single trading day — the largest one-day percentage decline in Dow Jones history — with the crash spreading to markets in Europe and Asia within hours.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell about 22.6% in one session; global markets lost a comparable share of their value over the following days.

historical_precedents.what_happened: US stock indices plunged roughly 9% in minutes before recovering most of the loss within the same session, driven in part by high-frequency algorithmic trading; regulators later introduced new circuit-breaker rules.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Dow Jones dropped about 1,000 points intraday before rebounding; the episode led to new exchange-wide trading-halt rules.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Global stock markets fell roughly a third from their peak over about a month as the pandemic spread and lockdowns began, triggering multiple single-day trading halts.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The S&P 500 fell about 34% peak-to-trough; US markets hit circuit-breaker halts on four separate days in March 2020.

historical_precedents.what_happened: China's newly introduced circuit-breaker mechanism, only in effect for its fourth trading day, halted trading twice within minutes of the open after the CSI 300 index fell 7% — the second halt shut the market for the rest of the day in under 30 minutes; regulators suspended the mechanism entirely days later after concluding it had accelerated the sell-off rather than calmed it.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Shanghai Composite fell roughly 7% in a single session on two separate days that week; the panic spread to European and US markets, contributing to one of the worst opening weeks for global stocks on record.

possibility_chains: A sudden, deep single-day stock decline has historically prompted exchanges to briefly halt trading via circuit breakers, giving markets time to reassess before reopening.
Sharp equity sell-offs have historically pushed investors toward traditionally safer assets such as government bonds and gold, temporarily lowering their yields and raising their prices.
Central banks have historically responded to severe market routs with public reassurances, emergency rate moves, or liquidity support when the decline threatened broader financial stability.
Extended market downturns have historically led companies to delay planned IPOs, financing rounds, and mergers until conditions stabilized.

playbooks.name: Bank failure and contagion fears

historical_precedents.what_happened: The investment bank filed for the largest bankruptcy in US history after a rapid loss of confidence and a failed rescue search, freezing credit markets worldwide and accelerating the global financial crisis.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Global stock markets fell sharply in the following weeks; credit markets froze, and governments worldwide launched emergency bank bailout and guarantee programs.

historical_precedents.event: Silicon Valley Bank and Credit Suisse
historical_precedents.what_happened: Silicon Valley Bank failed within days after a bond-portfolio loss triggered a rapid deposit run — the largest US bank failure since 2008 — followed days later by UBS's emergency takeover of Credit Suisse; regulators moved quickly to guarantee deposits and shore up confidence.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Regional bank stocks fell sharply for weeks; US and Swiss authorities announced emergency deposit-guarantee and liquidity measures within days.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Hokkaido Takushoku Bank became the first major Japanese bank to fail since World War II, followed days later by the collapse of Yamaichi Securities, then Japan's fourth-largest brokerage — both undone by bad loans and hidden losses accumulated since the bursting of Japan's asset-price bubble.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Nikkei fell sharply in the following sessions and Japan's banking sector entered a prolonged credit crunch; the failures deepened fears about hidden bad debt across Japan's banking system and fed into the broader Asian financial crisis unfolding at the same time.

possibility_chains: A single bank failure has historically triggered deposit runs at other banks perceived to share similar risks, even when those banks were otherwise healthy.
Bank-sector stress has historically prompted central banks and regulators to open emergency lending facilities or guarantee deposits to restore confidence.
Periods of banking contagion fear have historically tightened credit availability for businesses and consumers more broadly, beyond the institutions directly affected.
Regulators have historically arranged rapid emergency takeovers or backstops of failing banks to prevent a wider loss of confidence in the banking system.

playbooks.name: Volcanic ash cloud grounds flights

historical_precedents.what_happened: Ash from the Icelandic volcano drifted over European airspace, grounding roughly 100,000 flights over about a week — the largest air-travel shutdown in Europe since World War II — because ash particles can damage jet engines.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Airlines lost an estimated several billion dollars in revenue; millions of passengers were stranded worldwide.

historical_precedents.event: British Airways Flight 9 volcanic ash encounter, Indonesia
historical_precedents.what_happened: A passenger jet flying near Mount Galunggung in Indonesia lost power in all four engines after flying through an invisible ash cloud from the erupting volcano; the crew managed to restart the engines and land safely, but the incident was the first widely publicized demonstration that volcanic ash could disable jet aircraft mid-flight.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The incident directly led to the creation of the international Volcanic Ash Advisory Centre system that airlines and regulators still rely on today to route flights around ash clouds — the same system invoked during the 2010 Eyjafjallajökull shutdown.

possibility_chains: A volcanic ash cloud over major air routes has historically grounded flights for days until ash concentrations were confirmed safe for jet engines.
Extended flight groundings have historically stranded large numbers of passengers and disrupted connecting itineraries well beyond the airspace directly affected.
Prolonged airspace closures have historically cost airlines billions of dollars in lost revenue and passenger compensation.
Time-sensitive air cargo, including perishable goods, has historically been delayed or spoiled during extended volcanic groundings.

playbooks.name: Major cloud or software outage

historical_precedents.what_happened: A mistyped command during routine maintenance took a large portion of Amazon's cloud storage service offline for several hours, disrupting thousands of websites and apps that depend on it.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Analysts at the time estimated the outage cost S&P 500 companies alone over $100 million during the disruption window.

historical_precedents.what_happened: A faulty update to widely used cybersecurity software caused mass crashes on Windows computers worldwide, grounding flights and disrupting hospitals, banks, and broadcasters in one of the largest IT outages on record.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Delta Air Lines alone estimated the disruption cost it around $500 million; thousands of flights were cancelled globally over several days.

possibility_chains: An outage at a major cloud or widely used software provider has historically cascaded to any business or service built on top of it, regardless of that business's own reliability.
Large-scale IT outages have historically disrupted airlines, hospitals, and financial services simultaneously, since many rely on the same small number of dominant providers.
Major outages have historically renewed public and regulatory scrutiny of concentration risk in cloud and software infrastructure.
Affected companies have historically faced significant remediation costs and, in some cases, compensation claims following major outages.

playbooks.name: Disease outbreak with international health emergency

historical_precedents.what_happened: The World Health Organization declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern as Ebola spread across Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone; several airlines suspended routes to the region and some countries imposed travel restrictions.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The World Bank estimated the outbreak cost the three hardest-hit countries over $2 billion in lost economic output.

historical_precedents.what_happened: The WHO declared a Public Health Emergency as Zika spread across the Americas, linked to birth defects; several countries issued travel advisories affecting tourism, notably ahead of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Tourism-dependent economies in affected regions reported measurable declines in visitor bookings during the advisory period.

possibility_chains: A WHO-declared public health emergency has historically triggered travel advisories and, in some cases, temporary route suspensions by airlines.
Disease outbreaks with international travel warnings have historically reduced tourism and business travel to the affected region for the duration of the advisory.
Outbreaks affecting agricultural or manufacturing regions have historically disrupted local labor supply and production.
Vaccine and treatment developers have historically seen increased investment and expedited regulatory review during major outbreaks.

playbooks.name: Major manufacturer labor strike

historical_precedents.event: UAW strike against Detroit's Big Three automakers
historical_precedents.what_happened: The United Auto Workers union staged the first simultaneous strike against General Motors, Ford, and Stellantis in its history, idling multiple assembly plants over about six weeks before reaching new contracts.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Contemporary economic analyses estimated the strike cost the US economy several billion dollars in lost output, wages, and dealer revenue combined.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Tens of thousands of garment workers walked out and blocked highways across industrial zones near Dhaka demanding a tripled minimum wage, forcing hundreds of factories supplying major Western clothing brands to shut temporarily; police clashes with strikers left several workers dead.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Factory closures disrupted production for brands including H&M, Zara, and Levi's, which source heavily from Bangladesh — the world's second-largest garment exporter after China — and the government ultimately raised the minimum wage by about 56%, still short of union demands.

possibility_chains: A strike halting a major manufacturer's production lines has historically delayed vehicle or product deliveries for the duration of the stoppage.
Extended manufacturing strikes have historically weighed on the affected company's near-term earnings and stock price.
A strike settlement at one major manufacturer has historically influenced wage demands at competitors and in other industries.
Suppliers dependent on a struck plant's output have historically faced their own layoffs or production cuts during extended strikes.

playbooks.name: Government funding shutdown

historical_precedents.what_happened: A funding impasse over border wall spending led to a 35-day partial shutdown — the longest in US history — furloughing hundreds of thousands of federal workers and delaying government economic data releases.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The Congressional Budget Office estimated the shutdown cost the US economy about $3 billion in permanently lost output.

historical_precedents.what_happened: A 16-day shutdown over healthcare-law funding furloughed roughly 800,000 federal workers and delayed the release of economic indicators including the monthly jobs report.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Standard & Poor's estimated the shutdown shaved several billion dollars off US economic output.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Deep divisions between Belgium's Dutch-speaking and French-speaking parties after an inconclusive June 2010 election left the country without a fully empowered elected government for 541 days — widely reported at the time as a record for a country not in a civil war. A caretaker government kept spending under the previous budget while coalition talks dragged on.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Belgium's borrowing costs rose during the deadlock, and S&P downgraded the country's credit rating in November 2011, citing the political impasse alongside its high debt load; a coalition finally formed and passed a new budget in December 2011.

possibility_chains: A government shutdown or prolonged inability to form a government has historically delayed the release of official economic data that markets and businesses rely on for decision-making.
Extended shutdowns have historically reduced near-term economic growth via furloughed government workers and paused public contracts.
Government contractors and public employees have historically faced delayed pay and disrupted operations during prolonged funding lapses.
Prolonged budget or coalition-formation standoffs have historically weighed on consumer and business confidence surveys, and in some cases raised a country's borrowing costs.

playbooks.name: Major wildfire disaster

historical_precedents.what_happened: The deadliest and most destructive wildfire in California history destroyed the town of Paradise and killed dozens of people; the fire was later linked to utility equipment, and the responsible utility filed for bankruptcy the following year.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Insured losses were estimated at over $10 billion; the utility company later filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy citing wildfire liabilities.

historical_precedents.what_happened: An unusually intense bushfire season burned tens of millions of hectares across Australia, destroying thousands of homes and blanketing major cities in smoke for weeks.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Australian government and insurer estimates put total economic losses in the tens of billions of Australian dollars.

historical_precedents.event: Canadian wildfire smoke over the US Northeast
historical_precedents.what_happened: Record wildfire activity across Canada sent smoke plumes over major US East Coast cities including New York, prompting air-quality alerts and flight delays.
historical_precedents.market_impact: New York recorded some of the worst air-quality readings of any major city in the world during the peak smoke days.

historical_precedents.what_happened: A sharp surge in fires linked to illegal deforestation swept the Brazilian Amazon, drawing international alarm over the loss of the world's largest rainforest and a major global carbon sink; smoke darkened skies over São Paulo, thousands of kilometers away.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Several European countries threatened to block the EU-Mercosur trade agreement over the fires, some international investors and companies reassessed sourcing tied to Brazilian deforestation, and the episode intensified pressure on commodity supply chains linked to cattle and soy production in the region.

possibility_chains: Large wildfires have historically forced evacuations and destroyed homes, businesses, and agricultural land across the fire's path.
Wildfire smoke has historically degraded air quality far beyond the fire itself, at times affecting cities hundreds of miles away.
Utilities found responsible for causing major wildfires have historically faced significant financial and legal liabilities, in at least one case leading to bankruptcy.
Repeated severe wildfire seasons have historically raised property insurance costs and reduced insurance availability in the affected regions.
